case_name: Potable reuse from contaminated surface water with ricin barrier needs and lithium control under high-recovery limits
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for surface-water-derived potable reuse challenged by ricin and lithium, balancing pathogen/toxin barrier performance with lithium reduction, high recovery, and constrained concentrate handling.

input_data.scenario_background: A utility is upgrading an advanced water purification facility that blends highly treated municipal effluent with an impaired surface water source during drought periods for indirect potable reuse. Monitoring has identified a low-level ricin-related contamination concern requiring robust toxin and bioburden barriers, while lithium is persistently elevated enough to trigger a local finished-water target. Several routes are technically capable, but the utility must maintain very high water recovery and has limited options for managing large membrane concentrate volumes, creating a trade-off between broad contaminant rejection and residuals burden.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should provide a robust multi-barrier potable reuse train with effectively non-detect ricin risk and lithium reduced to a locally imposed operational target below 0.2 mg/L while sustaining stable compliance.
secondary_goals: maintain defensible multiple barriers for acute toxin incidents
control DBP precursor carryover and fouling risk

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: concentrate handling capacity is limited; avoid large brine volumes and options requiring difficult off-site hazardous liquid residual management
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: train must tolerate seasonal surface water quality swings and provide resilient barriers during short-term contamination spikes

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulates and reduce organic loading to protect downstream advanced treatment
core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_biologically_active_oxidati: decrease fouling precursors and improve overall treatment robustness
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide a strong particulate and macromolecule barrier including ricin-associated solids or aggregates
core_unit_functions.high_recovery_reverse_osmosis: achieve primary lithium rejection and broad dissolved contaminant reduction at optimized recovery
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: add an additional advanced potable reuse barrier for trace organics and overall process redundancy
core_unit_functions.permeate_stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry for distribution or recharge compatibility
core_unit_functions.small_volume_brine_crystallization_or_zl: manage the reduced but still present concentrate stream under constrained disposal conditions

applicability_rationale: lithium is a small monovalent ion that is not effectively removed by conventional clarification or adsorption, making reverse osmosis the most defensible core step for meeting the sub-0.2 mg/L target
ricin is a protein toxin for which a multi-barrier train with solids removal, membrane separation, and downstream advanced treatment is appropriate for potable reuse risk management

a high-recovery RO design with strong pretreatment best balances lithium compliance against the stated need to limit concentrate volume rather than simply maximizing rejection with conventional lower-recovery RO
this route is expensive and operationally significant, but it offers the strongest compliance margin for both contaminants in one integrated train
potential_risks: even optimized high-recovery RO still generates a brine stream that requires careful management
lithium rejection can vary with membrane selection, recovery, and temperature, so pilot confirmation is needed
surface water organic matter and scaling species can constrain recovery if pretreatment is unstable
capital and operator competency requirements are substantial

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce suspended solids and part of the organic load
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide an added adsorptive safety factor for organic toxin-related material and taste-odor precursors
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: serve as a robust barrier for particles and protein-associated contaminants
core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_reversal: remove dissolved ions including lithium with potentially higher water recovery than RO for some feed waters
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide disinfection and additional reuse barrier support

applicability_rationale: electrodialysis-based desalting can be attractive where high water recovery and reduced hydraulic concentrate volume are more important than maximizing rejection of every dissolved constituent
the concentrate stream from EDR is often smaller and operationally different from RO brine, which can better align with the concentrate handling constraint
UF plus PAC provides a credible front-end barrier approach for ricin-related concerns while lowering fouling risk to ion-selective desalting equipment
this route ranks below RO because lithium removal to a stringent finished-water target is less universally demonstrated and more water-matrix dependent than with RO
potential_risks: lithium removal may be insufficient to reliably achieve below 0.2 mg/L without careful staging or polishing
EDR performance is sensitive to scaling control and membrane stack management
PAC solids handling adds residual management duties
ricin destruction is not the primary role of this train, so reliance is on barrier removal and upstream control

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: reduce natural organic matter and particulate load
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: protect downstream membranes and provide particulate/protein barrier removal
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: partially remove dissolved ions and organic contaminants with lower pressure than RO
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: provide an advanced oxidation barrier and potable reuse polishing step

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration offers a plausible compromise route when the utility wants some dissolved contaminant reduction with lower pressure and potentially better energy performance than RO
UF plus NF plus UV-AOP forms a defensible reuse-oriented barrier train for ricin-related concerns
this option ranks lower because lithium, as a small monovalent ion, is often only modestly rejected by NF relative to RO, making attainment of a stringent lithium target uncertain
it is technically capable for overall water quality improvement but misaligned with the lithium-specific effluent requirement
potential_risks: lithium breakthrough is the central compliance risk
NF still produces concentrate, though often somewhat less severe than full RO systems
high DOC surface water can drive fouling if coagulation control is inconsistent
utility may need a polishing step or blending restrictions if lithium target is strict

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and a portion of organic matter
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_biological_fil: adsorb and biologically polish dissolved organic matter and improve overall water quality
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide a physical barrier for particles and macromolecules including proteinaceous contaminants
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: add advanced oxidation and reuse polishing

applicability_rationale: this non-concentrate route aligns very well with the high-recovery and concentrate-management constraints because it avoids brine generation
for ricin-related concerns, clarification, GAC biofiltration, UF, and UV-AOP create multiple barriers and are operationally familiar in many reuse settings
it ranks below membrane-desalting options because lithium removal is expected to be poor; conventional adsorption and oxidation are not reliable primary controls for dissolved lithium
this option is attractive only if the lithium target were relaxed or managed by source control rather than treatment
potential_risks: finished water lithium is likely to remain near influent levels
GAC replacement or reactivation logistics still require planning
ricin oxidation efficacy in full-scale practice is less straightforward than established microbial inactivation metrics, so barrier credit must be conservatively assigned
seasonal DOC spikes may shorten carbon life

core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide robust pretreatment and macromolecule removal
core_unit_functions.dual_pass_reverse_osmosis: maximize dissolved contaminant rejection including lithium
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: provide advanced reuse polishing and redundancy
core_unit_functions.evaporation_pond_or_offsite_brine_dispos: manage the larger concentrate stream from conservative membrane operation

applicability_rationale: this route is technically the most aggressive dissolved contaminant control option and could provide the greatest lithium reduction margin
it is intentionally ranked low because it conflicts with the case constraints: dual-pass RO generally lowers net recovery, increases energy demand, and worsens concentrate handling burden
while highly capable on treatment performance, it is a poor strategic fit where water recovery is a priority and brine management options are limited
it serves as a technically capable but constraint-misaligned alternative
potential_risks: large brine volume and disposal cost may be prohibitive
recovery losses conflict with drought-resilience objectives
high scaling and fouling control demands increase operator burden
project may be difficult to permit or sustain economically

preferred_rank_pattern: RO-based trains should appear near the top because lithium control is the decisive dissolved-contaminant challenge
A high-recovery or reduced-brine desalting route should outrank more brute-force low-recovery membrane options
Non-concentrate conventional trains may appear as plausible alternatives for ricin barriers but should rank lower because they do not reliably remove lithium
At least one technically strong membrane option should rank lower due to poor fit with water-recovery and concentrate constraints

must_reflect: high water recovery is a major decision driver rather than only contaminant removal efficiency
concentrate handling limitations should downgrade otherwise strong membrane options
potable reuse requires multiple barriers and not only a single contaminant-specific unit process

must_contain: why lithium is difficult to remove without desalting-type processes
why ricin as a protein toxin supports strong physical barrier and advanced treatment logic in potable reuse
why high-recovery RO or alternative high-recovery desalting can be favored over conventional lower-recovery high-rejection systems
why adsorption and conventional clarification routes are not sufficient primary lithium controls

should_mention: fouling and scaling risk under surface water variability
need for conservative barrier credit for acute toxin incidents
